title: Draba lonchocarpa
intro: Draba lonchocarpa, also known as long-fruited whitlow-grass, is a small, annual or biennial plant species belonging to the Brassicaceae family. Traditionally, Draba lonchocarpa has been used to treat various health issues, including inflammation and digestive problems, due to its purported health benefits. The bioactive constituents of this plant include flavonoids, alkaloids, and saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, Draba lonchocarpa is commonly prepared as a tincture or infused in tea form, and its extracts are also used in capsules or tablets to leverage its potential health benefits.
taxonomy: Draba lonchocarpa is classified within the family Brassicaceae, commonly known as the mustard or cabbage family. This family includes various species of flowering plants that are often found in temperate regions. Draba lonchocarpa is a member of the genus Draba, which is a group of small, herbaceous plants typically found in alpine and subalpine environments. The species is further classified within the subfamily Brassicoideae, a subfamily of the Brassicaceae family. As a member of the Brassicaceae family, Draba lonchocarpa exhibits characteristics such as four-petaled flowers and a cruciferous leaf structure.
